  "opinion": "I've been following the news lately, and it's frustrating how much fake stuff seems to be floating around online. I remember when we first got dial-up internet back in the day – that was a simpler time, you know? Anyway, I don't spend too much time on social media these days; just enough to keep up with what's going on in my family and friends' lives.\n\nI think dealing with fake content is like trying to figure out which wrench is real from all the duds. My dad always taught me that you have to be careful about what you're using because it can cause problems down the line. So, if I see something suspicious online – say a post claiming to be from my old mechanic shop but with some weird grammar errors – I try not to jump on the bandwagon right away.\n\nI believe one way is to verify information before sharing or believing it completely. That sounds reasonable enough. My brother-in-law has an office job, and he talks about using fact-checking tools all day long – probably something like Snopes or PolitiFact? I don't know much about that stuff myself because my education only took me up to lower secondary, but maybe there are apps for people like me who aren't so tech-savvy.\n\nAnother thing is supporting credible sources. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been following the news lately, and it's frustrating how much fake stuff seems to be floating around online. I remember when we first got dial-up internet back in the day – that was a simpler time, you know? Anyway, I don't spend too much time on social media these days; just enough to keep up with what's going on in my family and friends' lives.

I think dealing with fake content is like trying to figure out which wrench is real from all the duds. My dad always taught me that you have to be careful about what you're using because it can cause problems down the line. So, if I see something suspicious online – say a post claiming to be from my old mechanic shop but with some weird grammar errors – I try not to jump on the bandwagon right away.

I believe one way is to verify information before sharing or believing it completely. That sounds reasonable enough. My brother-in-law has an office job, and he talks about using fact-checking tools all day long – probably something like Snopes or PolitiFact? I don't know much about that stuff myself because my education only took me up to lower secondary, but maybe there are apps for people like me who aren't so tech-savvy.

Another thing is supporting credible sources. My church always encourages us to be careful with what we read and share – they talk about discerning truth from falsehood these days more than ever before. So yeah, sticking close to established news outlets might help filter out the nonsense.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1. From my background as a mechanic with high conscientiousness (score:88), I understand the importance of verifying tools and information.
2. My low extraversion means I prefer cautious approaches over being overly opinionated, aligning with my father's teachings about careful work.
3. As a curious person but not highly educated in media literacy, I rely on simpler methods like checking sources rather than complex verification techniques.
